background: Acute lower respiratory tract infection is a leading cause of death among infants and under-5s in developing countries. A simple test is needed to differentiate between infants with acute respiratory infection who have lower respiratory tract infection (LRI) and require antibiotics, and those with upper respiratory tract infection (URI). The study explores the usefulness of respiratory rate as a diagnostic tool for this purpose.

task.query: Construct $2 \times 2$ tables for each of the four cut-offs (30, 40, 50, and 60 breaths/min) relating low and high respiratory rate to the correct classification (LRI or URI). Which cut-off gives the best balance of sensitivity and specificity (where their sum is maximized)?
task.answer:
Taking the cut-off as respiratory rates less than or equal to 30, 40, 50, or 60 breaths/min gives:

| Cut-off | Sensitivity | Specificity |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 30      | 99%         | 11%         |
| 40      | 96%         | 62%         |
| 50      | 89%         | 92%         |
| 60      | 61%         | 98%         |

The best cut-off is 50 breaths/min, with an overall correct assessment for 91% (266/293) of infants.

task.query: Given that the prevalence of LRI among all infants with acute respiratory infection in a developing country is 3%, which cut-off gives the best balance of positive and negative predictive values (PPV and NPV) when the prevalence is 3%?
task.answer:
Using the formulae for PPV and NPV:

| Cut-off | PPV   | NPV   |
|---------|-------|-------|
| 30      | 3.3%  | 99.8% |
| 40      | 7%    | 99.8% |
| 50      | 26%   | 99.6% |
| 60      | 49%   | 98.8% |

With low prevalence, NPV is usually not helpful. The PPV increases as the cut-off level rises. The 'best' choice to maximize correct predictions is a cut-off of 60 breaths/min. However, with this cut-off, nearly half of the infants with LRI would be missed. A cut-off of 50 breaths/min would identify nearly all infants with LRI but result in more false positives compared to a cut-off of 60 breaths/min.

task.query: If a respiratory rate of >50 breaths/min is taken as an indication of LRI and all such children are treated with antibiotics, what proportion of treated infants will have been treated unnecessarily? What proportion of LRI infants would not get antibiotics?
task.answer: The PPV at a cut-off of >50 breaths/min is 26%, meaning that 74% of treated infants will have been treated unnecessarily. The sensitivity at this cut-off is 89%, meaning that 89% of LRI infants would receive antibiotics, while 11% would not get antibiotics. At a lower cut-off of 40 breaths/min, the proportion of LRI infants treated with antibiotics would be higher (96%), but only 7% of those treated would have LRI. The choice of cut-off must consider non-statistical factors.

task.query: If general practitioners currently treat 80% of infants with respiratory tract infection (LRI or URI) with antibiotics, what would be the effect on antibiotic usage of the policy suggested in (c)?
task.answer:
If all infants above the cut-off of >50 breaths/min are treated, 89% of LRI infants and 8% of URI infants would be treated. Assuming a prevalence of LRI as 3%, the proportion treated would be:

0.89 × 0.03 + 0.08 × 0.97 = 0.10.

Thus, only 10% of infants would be treated with antibiotics, representing a significant cost saving. Using a cut-off of 40 breaths/min, the proportion treated would be:

0.96 × 0.03 + 0.38 × 0.97 = 0.40.

This would still cost half as much as the current policy.

data:
\begin{tabular}{|l|l|l|}
\hline Respiratory rate (breaths/min) & Number of children (\%) \\
\hline & LRI & URI \\
\hline 0-30 & 1 (1\%) & 16 (11\%) \\
\hline 31-40 & 4 (3\%) & 77 (51\%) \\
\hline 41-50 & 10 (7\%) & 46 (30\%) \\
\hline 51-60 & 41 (29\%) & 9 (6\%) \\
\hline 61+ & 86 (61\%) & 3 (2\%) \\
\hline Total & 142 (100\%) & 151 (100\%) \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
